name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
لَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا نُوحًا إِلَىٰ قَوْمِهِۦ فَقَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ مَا لَكُم مِّنْ إِلَـٰهٍ غَيْرُهُۥٓ إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ عَلَيْكُمْ عَذَابَ يَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ (٥٩)
```

```english
Certainly We sent Noah to his people. He said, ‘O my people, worship Allah! You have no other god besides Him. Indeed I fear for you the punishment of a tremendous day.’ (59)
```

```arabic
قَالَ ٱلْمَلَأُ مِن قَوْمِهِۦٓ إِنَّا لَنَرَىٰكَ فِى ضَلَـٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ (٦٠)
```

```english
The elite of his people said, ‘Indeed we see you in manifest error.’ (60)
```

```arabic
قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ لَيْسَ بِى ضَلَـٰلَةٌ وَلَـٰكِنِّى رَسُولٌ مِّن رَّبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٦١)
```

```english
He said, ‘O my people, I am not in error. Rather, I am an apostle from the Lord of all the worlds. (61)
```

```arabic
أُبَلِّغُكُمْ رِسَـٰلَـٰتِ رَبِّى وَأَنصَحُ لَكُمْ وَأَعْلَمُ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ مَا لَا تَعْلَمُونَ (٦٢)
```

```english
I communicate to you the messages of my Lord, and I am your well-wisher, and I know from Allah what you do not know. (62)
```

```arabic
أَوَعَجِبْتُمْ أَن جَآءَكُمْ ذِكْرٌ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ عَلَىٰ رَجُلٍ مِّنكُمْ لِيُنذِرَكُمْ وَلِتَتَّقُوا۟ وَلَعَلَّكُمْ تُرْحَمُونَ (٦٣)
```

```english
Do you consider it odd that a reminder from your Lord should come to you through a man from among yourselves, to warn you so that you may be Godwary and so that you may receive His mercy?’ (63)
```

```arabic
فَكَذَّبُوهُ فَأَنجَيْنَـٰهُ وَٱلَّذِينَ مَعَهُۥ فِى ٱلْفُلْكِ وَأَغْرَقْنَا ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَآ ۚ إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ قَوْمًا عَمِينَ (٦٤)
```

```english
But they denied him. So We delivered him and those who were with him in the ark, and We drowned those who denied Our signs. Indeed, they were a blind lot. (64)
```

## (Commentary)

This is a follow-up to the preceding invitation to monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — and the prohibition against associating partners with Allah, the Glorified, and the denial of His signs. It does so by recounting the story of Nuh (peace be upon him) and his being sent to his people to call them to the oneness of Allah and to abandon the worship of others. It describes the denial he faced from the generality of his people and their persistence in accusing him of lying, whereupon Allah sent the flood upon them, saving Nuh and those who believed with him, and then utterly destroying the remainder to the last man. Allah then followed his story with several accounts of His messengers, such as Hud, Salih, Shu’ayb, Lut, and Musa (peace be upon them), for this very same purpose.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **لَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا نُوحًا إِلَىٰ قَوْمِهِ** **"Certainly We sent Noah to his people..."** (7:59) until the end of the verse. Allah, the Glorified, began with his story, as he is the first messenger whose detailed account Allah mentions in the Quran, just as the detailed discussion of his story will follow in Surah Hud, if Allah, the Exalted, wills.

The letter *lam* (اللام) in His saying: **لَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا نُوحًا** is for an oath (للقسم), employed here for emphasis (التأكيد) because the discourse is directed at the polytheists who deny prophethood (النبوة). His saying: **فَقَالَ يَا قَوْمِ اعْبُدُوا اللَّهَ مَا لَكُم مِّنْ إِلَٰهٍ غَيْرُهُ** **"...and he said, ‘O my people, worship Allah! You have no other god besides Him.’"** (7:59). He addressed them saying, "O my people," thereby attributing them to himself so that his speech would proceed in accordance with the sincere counsel (النصح) that he would later inform them of regarding himself. He called them, first and foremost, to the monotheism (التوحيد) of Allah, the Exalted; for he called them to His worship and informed them of the non-existence of any deity other than Him. Thus, it is a call to worship Allah alone without associating anything else with Him in His worship, which is monotheism (التوحيد).

Then he warned them with his saying: **إِنِّي أَخَافُ عَلَيْكُمْ عَذَابَ يَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ** **"‘Indeed I fear for you the punishment of a tremendous day.’"** (7:59). Its apparent meaning refers to the Day of Resurrection; thus, this includes a call to two of the principles of religion (أصول الدين), namely monotheism (التوحيد) and the Return (المعاد) — *the resurrection of the dead for judgment*. As for the third principle, which is prophethood (النبوة), he would explicitly state it in his saying: **يَا قَوْمِ لَيْسَ بِي ضَلَالَةٌ وَلَٰكِنِّي رَسُولٌ** **"He said, ‘O my people, there is no error in me, but I am an apostle...’"** (7:61).

Furthermore, the very nature of the call—which is an invitation to a type of worship they did not recognize—as well as the warning of something they did not know—namely the punishment of the Resurrection—serves as an intimation of a message (الرسالة) from the One to Whom he calls. Evidence for this is found in his response to them: **أَوَعَجِبْتُمْ أَن جَاءَكُمْ ذِكْرٌ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ عَلَىٰ رَجُلٍ مِّنكُمْ لِيُنذِرَكُمْ** **"Do you marvel that a reminder should come to you from your Lord through a man from among you, that he may warn you..."** (7:63). This indicates their wonderment at his message upon hearing the very first words he addressed to them in his call, which was his saying: **يَا قَوْمِ اعْبُدُوا اللَّهَ مَا لَكُم مِّنْ إِلَٰهٍ غَيْرُهُ** **"‘O my people, worship Allah! You have no other god besides Him.’"**

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قَالَ الْمَلَأُ مِن قَوْمِهِ إِنَّا لَنَرَاكَ فِي ضَلَالٍ مُّبِينٍ** **"The elite of his people said, ‘Indeed we see you in manifest error.’"** (7:60). The **elite** (الملأ) are the nobles and the prominent figures of the people; they are so named because they "fill" (يملئون) hearts with awe and eyes with beauty and adornment. They only accused him of manifest error (الضلال المبين) and emphasized it so strongly because they did not expect an objector to challenge them by calling for the rejection of their gods and the directing of worship toward Allah, the Glorified, through a message and a warning. They marveled at this and thus emphasized his "error," claiming that this was the very essence of error itself. The "seeing" (الرؤية) here is seeing according to thought, meaning judgment (الحكم).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قَالَ يَا قَوْمِ لَيْسَ بِي ضَلَالَةٌ** **"He said, ‘O my people, there is no error in me...’"** (7:61) until the end of the verse. He answered them by negating error from himself and following it with the corrective statement that he is a messenger from Allah, the Glorified. He mentioned Him with His attribute, **رَبِّ الْعَالَمِينَ** **"the Lord of all the worlds,"** in order to consolidate all Lordship (الربوبية) for Him, in contrast to their division of it among their various gods by specializing each with certain affairs and domains, such as the lordship of the sea, the lordship of the land, the lordship of the earth, the lordship of the sky, and so on.

He (peace be upon him) stripped his response of emphasis to signal the self-evidence of his message and the absence of his error, in the face of their persistence in that regard and the emphasis of their claim.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أُبَلِّغُكُمْ رِسَالَاتِ رَبِّي وَأَنصَحُ لَكُمْ وَأَعْلَمُ مِنَ اللَّهِ مَا لَا تَعْلَمُونَ** **"‘I deliver to you the messages of my Lord and advise you, and I know from Allah what you do not know.’"** (7:62). He informed them of his own attributes, explaining that he delivers the messages of his Lord; this is the function of the message (الرسالة) and its immediate, necessary implication. The use of the plural "messages" (رسالات) indicates that they are numerous and that he has objectives his Lord commanded him to convey beyond monotheism and the Return, for he is a prophet and a messenger from the "Arch-Messengers" (أولي العزم) — *the messengers of strong resolve who brought new divine laws* — and the possessor of a Book and a Law (الشريعة).

Then he mentioned that he advises them (ينصح لكم), which refers to his exhortations through warning and glad tidings to bring them closer to the obedience of their Lord and distance them from arrogance and disdain for His servitude (العبودية). All of this is by mentioning what Allah has made known to him regarding the beginning of creation and its return, and His established ways (سننه) — *the consistent divine laws* — governing it. For this reason, he mentioned thirdly that he knows from Allah what they do not know, such as the events of the Day of Resurrection regarding reward and punishment and other matters, and the consequences of obedience and disobedience in terms of Allah's pleasure and His wrath, and the various forms of His blessings and His retributions.

From this, it becomes clear that each of the three clauses is intended for a specific purpose—namely his saying: **أُبَلِّغُكُمْ** (I deliver...), **وَأَنصَحُ لَكُمْ** (and I advise you...), and **وَأَعْلَمُ** (and I know...). These are three consecutive attributes, not as some have said: that the first two are attributes and the third is a circumstantial clause (جملة حالية) referring to the subject of "and I advise you."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَوَعَجِبْتُمْ أَن جَاءَكُمْ ذِكْرٌ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ** **"Do you marvel that a reminder should come to you from your Lord..."** (7:63) until the end of the verse. This is a rhetorical question of disapproval (استفهام إنكاري) denouncing their wonderment at his claim of the message and his calling them to the true religion. What is meant by the "reminder" (الذكر) is that by which Allah is remembered, which are the true gnoses (المعارف) that were revealed to him. His saying: **مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ** "from your Lord" is connected to an omitted word, meaning "a reminder originating from your Lord."

His saying: **لِيُنذِرَكُمْ** "that he may warn you," **وَلِتَتَّقُوا** "and that you may be God-conscious," and **وَلَعَلَّكُمْ تُرْحَمُونَ** "and so that you may receive [His] mercy" are all connected to His saying: **جَاءَكُمْ** "should come to you." The meaning is: for the purpose that the messenger may warn you, and that you may become God-conscious (تتقوا), leading to the hope that the divine mercy may encompass you. For although godwariness (التقوى) leads to salvation, it is not a sufficient cause (علة تامة) in itself. What has been recounted of the summary of his speech (peace be upon him) contains sublime divine gnoses.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَكَذَّبُوهُ فَأَنجَيْنَاهُ وَالَّذِينَ مَعَهُ فِي الْفُلْكِ** **"But they impugned him; so We delivered him and those who were with him in the ark..."** (7:64). The word *al-fulk* (الفلك) means the ship; it is used for both singular and plural as mentioned by al-Raghib, and it can be masculine or feminine as stated in *al-Sihah*. His saying: **قَوْمًا عَمِينَ** **"...a blind people"** (7:64) consists of a noun and its adjective. The word **blind** (عمين) is the plural of *ami* (عمي), like *khashin* (خشن); it is an intensive adjective (صفة مشبهة) derived from *amiya ya'ma* (عمي يعمى). *Ami* (العمي) is like *a'ma* (الأعمى), except that *ami* is specifically used for the blindness of insight (البصيرة), while *a'ma* is for the blindness of sight, as has been said. The meaning of the verse is clear.
